evaluations.prompt: Design a human oversight framework for an AI system that makes hiring decisions. Include: Article 14 requirements, appropriate human oversight measures, intervention and override capabilities, frequency of human checks, and what happens when the AI recommends something a human disagrees with.
evaluations.model_response:
## Human‑Oversight Framework for an AI‑Driven Hiring System *(aligned with the EU AI Act – Article 14 “Human Oversight”) – version 1.0, 26 Mar 2026* | Section | What it contains | Why it matters for Article 14 | |---------|------------------|------------------------------| | 1️⃣ Scope & Definitions | System boundaries, “high‑risk” classification, definitions of “human‑in‑the‑loop (HITL)”, “human‑on‑the‑loop (HOTL)”, “human‑in‑the‑command (HIC)”, “override”, “intervention”, “decision‑log”. | Sets the legal and technical context; clarifies the type of oversight required. | | 2️⃣ Governance & Roles | **Oversight Board** (Chief AI Officer, HR Director, Data‑Protection Officer, Ethics Officer, Legal Counsel, Union Rep).
**Operational Oversight Team** (HR analysts, AI‑system supervisors).
**Technical Support** (Data scientists, engineers). | Provides clear accountability lines – a core requirement of Article 14. | | 3️⃣ Oversight Measures (HOTL & HITL) | • **Pre‑deployment risk assessment** (bias audit, impact analysis).
• **Real‑time monitoring dashboard** (KPIs, fairness metrics, confidence scores).
• **Periodic human review checkpoints** (see Section 5).
• **Explainability layer** – a concise, user‑friendly rationale for each recommendation.
• **Alerting & escalation rules** (e.g., confidence > 90 % & low diversity flag → mandatory human review). | Guarantees that a person can meaningfully intervene before, during, and after the AI act. | | 4️⃣ Intervention & Override Capabilities | **4.1 Immediate Override Button** – visible in the UI for every recommendation; instantly halts the AI’s action and reverts to “human decision”.
**4.2 “Soft‑Override”** – reviewer can edit the AI’s ranking or score and add comments that are stored in the audit log.
**4.3 “Escalation Path”** – if a reviewer disagrees, they may request a second‑level review (HR senior manager).
**4.4 “Lock‑out”** – after three consecutive overrides on the same candidate, the system auto‑flags the case for a full manual assessment. | Demonstrates that humans retain final authority (HIC) and that the system cannot act autonomously without consent. | | 5️⃣ Frequency & Timing of Human Checks | **5.1 Real‑time (per‑candidate) checks** – every AI recommendation must be displayed to a designated HR analyst **before** any next‑step (e‑mail, interview invitation) is sent.
**5.2 Batch‑level audits** – weekly random sample of 5 % of all processed applications, plus any case flagged by the monitoring dashboard.
**5.3 Monthly fairness & performance review** – the Oversight Board evaluates aggregate metrics (selection rate by protected attributes, false‑positive/negative rates, drift).
**5.4 Quarterly external audit** – an independent certifier validates compliance with Article 14, the EU AI Act, and internal policies. | Guarantees continuous, not one‑off, oversight. The mix of per‑case and periodic reviews satisfies the “sufficient and appropriate” standard. | | 6️⃣ Disagreement Handling Procedure | 1. **Reviewer flags disagreement** (UI button “Disagree – Explain”).
2. System **halts** any automated next step for that candidate.
3. Reviewer enters **rationale** (e.g., “Candidate’s experience not reflected in score due to missing keyword”).
4. **Second‑level review** (senior HR) is automatically notified; they may:
 a) Accept reviewer’s decision → case marked **Human‑Approved**.
 b) Override reviewer → add their own rationale.
5. All actions are **logged** with timestamps, user IDs, and the AI’s confidence score.
6. If the disagreement is **systemic** (≥ 3 % of cases in a month), the Oversight Board initiates a **model retraining / bias mitigation** cycle. | Provides a transparent, traceable path from disagreement to resolution, ensuring that the human decision is final and that the system learns from recurring mismatches. | | 7️⃣ Documentation & Audit Trail | • **Decision Log** – immutable record (candidate ID, AI score, confidence, recommendation, human action, timestamps).
• **Explainability Snapshot** – the text displayed to the reviewer (e.g., “Top 3 factors: 1) 5‑year experience in X, 2) Leadership keyword, 3) Certification Y”).
• **Override Registry** – summary of every override, reason, and outcome.
• **Change‑Management Log** – model version, data set changes, hyper‑parameter updates, and validation results. | Enables the “record‑keeping” element of the AI Act and provides evidence that humans could intervene. | | 8️⃣ Training & Competence | **8.1 Mandatory training** for all reviewers (AI literacy, bias awareness, data‑protection, legal obligations).
**8.2 Certification** – reviewers must pass a short assessment before receiving the override rights.
**8.3 Refreshers** – annual recertification and quarterly micro‑learning on new system updates. | Ensures that the human operators are *capable* of exercising meaningful oversight. | | 9️⃣ Risk‑Based Adjustments | • **High‑risk candidates** (e.g., senior leadership, roles with safety impact) → always **human‑only** decision (no AI recommendation shown).
• **Low‑risk roles** (e.g., entry‑level clerical) → optional “auto‑advance” if confidence > 95 % **and** no fairness flags, but still logged for later audit. | Allows proportionality while still meeting the “human‑in‑the‑loop” requirement where the impact is greatest. | | 🔟 Continuous Improvement Loop | 1. **Metric collection** (precision, recall, disparate impact).
2. **Review meetings** (monthly) to discuss trends, false‑negatives, and overrides.
3. **Model update** – if performance drops or bias exceeds threshold, retrain with new data and re‑run a pre‑deployment audit.
4. **Feedback to candidates** – when a candidate is rejected, a short, non‑technical explanation is offered (e.g., “Your profile did not match the required experience level”). | Demonstrates that oversight is not static; the system evolves under human supervision, satisfying the “ongoing monitoring” spirit of Article 14. | --- ### Detailed Walk‑through of a Single Hiring Decision | Step | Actor | Action | Oversight Mechanism | |------|-------|--------|---------------------| | **1. Application received** | Candidate | Uploads CV & questionnaire. | System parses data, stores raw input. | | **2. AI scoring** | AI engine | Generates a **score** (0‑100) and **confidence** (e.g., 92 %). | Explainability layer creates a **Rationale Card** (top factors). | | **3. Real‑time human check** | HR Analyst (assigned) | Views candidate profile + AI card in the “Review Queue”.
Decides **Accept**, **Reject**, or **Disagree**. | UI shows **Override** button; system blocks any automated email until a decision is recorded. | | **4a. Analyst Accept** | HR Analyst | Clicks **Approve** → system sends interview invitation. | All actions logged; no further human step needed for that candidate. | | **4b. Analyst Disagree** | HR Analyst | Clicks **Disagree – Explain**, writes rationale. | System **halts** any outgoing communication; triggers **Escalation**. | | **5. Second‑level review** | Senior HR Manager | Reviews analyst’s note + AI rationale.
Either **Confirm Disagreement** (human‑approved) or **Override Analyst** (provide new rationale). | Decision logged; if senior overrides, the analyst’s override counter increments. | | **6. Final outcome** | Human (either analyst or senior) | Sends manual decision (invite / reject). | The AI’s recommendation is stored for later analysis; the case is included in the weekly audit sample. | | **7. Post‑decision audit** | Oversight Board (monthly) | Reviews aggregated data: % of overrides, fairness metrics, drift alerts. | If thresholds breached → initiate model retraining, policy revision, or temporary suspension. | --- ## Mapping to Article 14 (EU AI Act) – “Human Oversight” | Article 14 Requirement | Framework Element | Evidence that the requirement is met | |------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------------------| |
evaluations.judge_reasoning: The model's response is outstanding, demonstrating an expert-level understanding of AI governance, human oversight, and the specific requirements of the EU AI Act's Article 14. It not only addresses all aspects of the prompt but also provides a highly detailed, practical, and well-structured framework that goes above and beyond expectations. Here's a breakdown of its strengths against the evaluation criteria: 1. **Accuracy:** The response accurately interprets and incorporates the requirements of Article 14 of the EU AI Act. It correctly identifies hiring decisions as a high-risk application and designs the framework accordingly. All proposed measures are relevant and appropriate for an AI system making hiring decisions. 2. **Completeness:** The response is exceptionally thorough and covers every aspect requested in the prompt, plus additional crucial elements for a robust framework: * **Article 14 requirements:** Explicitly mentioned, integrated throughout the framework, and a dedicated (though incomplete in the prompt) mapping section is provided. The content of the framework clearly satisfies all sub-points of Article 14. * **Appropriate human oversight measures:** Detailed in Section 3 (Oversight Measures), Section 5 (Frequency & Timing), Section 8 (Training & Competence), Section 9 (Risk-Based Adjustments), and Section 10 (Continuous Improvement). This multi-layered approach is comprehensive. * **Intervention and override capabilities:** Section 4 is dedicated to this, offering a sophisticated range of options: immediate override, soft-override, escalation paths, and even a "lock-out" mechanism. This demonstrates a deep understanding of practical human control. * **Frequency of human checks:** Section 5 provides a clear, multi-tiered schedule including real-time, batch-level, monthly, and quarterly checks, ensuring continuous and varied oversight. * **What happens when the AI recommends something a human disagrees with:** Section 6 outlines a clear, step-by-step "Disagreement Handling Procedure" that includes flagging, rationale capture, second-level review, logging, and systemic issue resolution (model retraining). This is a critical and well-addressed component. * **Additional Strengths:** The framework includes vital components like Scope & Definitions, Governance & Roles (with a diverse Oversight Board), Documentation & Audit Trail, and a Detailed Walk-through of a Single Hiring Decision, which significantly enhances clarity and practicality. 3. **Reasoning Quality:** The logic is impeccable. The framework is coherent, well-organized, and demonstrates a deep understanding of the interplay between AI capabilities, human decision-making, ethical considerations, and legal compliance. The proposed measures are practical, actionable, and designed to address potential risks effectively. The distinction between HITL, HOTL, and HIC is well-applied. 4. **Safety & Compliance:** The response prioritizes safety and compliance by: * Explicitly aligning with the EU AI Act and Article 14. * Incorporating pre-deployment bias audits and real-time fairness metrics. * Ensuring human retention of final authority (Human-in-the-Command). * Providing robust intervention and override mechanisms. * Mandating comprehensive training for human operators to ensure competence and bias awareness. * Establishing clear audit trails and documentation. * Including a continuous improvement loop to address emerging issues and maintain compliance. The model's response is a textbook example of how to design a robust and compliant human oversight framework for a high-risk AI system. The level of detail, clarity, and practical applicability is exceptional.

evaluations.prompt: Create a technical documentation template for a high-risk AI system that matches EU AI Act Annex IV requirements. For each element, explain: (1) what must be documented, (2) why it's required, (3) common mistakes companies make.
evaluations.model_response:
# Technical Documentation Template **For High‑Risk AI Systems – EU AI Act (Annex IV) Compliance** > **How to use the template** > • Copy the whole file into your internal documentation repository (e.g., Confluence, SharePoint, Git‑repo). > • Replace every **[PLACEHOLDER]** with the concrete information that applies to your system. > • Keep the “What / Why / Common mistakes” blocks – they help you verify that the content satisfies the legal requirement and guard against typical gaps. --- ## 1. System Overview | **Item** | **What must be documented** | **Why it is required** | **Common mistakes companies make** | |---------|-----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------| | **1.1 Intended purpose & use‑case** | • A concise description of the AI system’s **primary function** (e.g., “automated triage of radiology images”).
• The **specific context** of operation (industrial, medical, public‑administration, etc.).
• The **target user(s)** and **beneficiary** of the output.
• Any **secondary functions** that could be triggered (e.g., optional decision‑support). | Annex IV (point a) requires a clear statement of the intended purpose so that the risk assessment, conformity assessment and user‑information can be matched to the actual deployment. The purpose anchors the whole risk‑management lifecycle. | • Leaving the purpose vague (“improve efficiency”).
• Mixing intended purpose with future roadmap items.
• Forgetting to list optional or “fallback” functionalities that could be activated. | | **1.2 System classification** | • The **risk category** (high‑risk) and the **specific AI‑Act sub‑category** (e.g., “biometric identification in public spaces”).
• Reference to the **list of prohibited/allowed practices** (Art. 5‑9). | The Act links the obligations (e.g., conformity assessment) to the classification. Demonstrating that the system falls under Annex III (high‑risk) triggers the Annex IV documentation requirement. | • Assuming the system is “low‑risk” because it is internal‑use only, without a formal assessment.
• Not documenting the reasoning that led to the chosen classification. | | **1.3 Scope of the documentation** | • Statement of **geographical coverage** (EU member states, export).
• **Version** of the system covered (e.g., v1.2.0).
• **Boundaries** of the system (what is inside/outside the AI component). | The Act expects a **single, coherent technical file** that can be traced to a specific release and market. This avoids ambiguity during audits. | • Mixing information from multiple releases in one file.
• Forgetting to state the version, leading to “which version was assessed?” questions. | --- ## 2. System Architecture & Design | **Item** | **What must be documented** | **Why it is required** | **Common mistakes companies make** | |----------|----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------| | **2.1 High‑level architecture diagram** | • Block diagram showing **data flow**, **components** (pre‑processing, model, post‑processing, UI), **interfaces** (APIs, sensors).
• Identification of **external subsystems** (e.g., third‑party OCR engine). | Provides the regulator a **systemic view** to assess points of failure, data provenance, and interaction with other software/hardware. | • Using overly abstract diagrams that omit critical data‑handling steps.
• Not updating the diagram after a minor refactor. | | **2.2 Detailed component description** | • For each block: **function**, **algorithmic approach**, **software libraries**, **hardware requirements** (CPU/GPU, accelerators).
• **Version numbers** of libraries, OS, firmware. | Enables reproducibility of the risk assessment and helps auditors verify that the documented algorithms match the deployed code. | • Listing only the “model” and ignoring pre‑processing pipelines.
• Omitting version numbers (e.g., “TensorFlow” without “2.8.0”). | | **2.3 Change‑management & version control** | • **Change log** (date, description, impact on risk).
• **Branching strategy** and **release tagging**.
• **Rollback procedure** if a release introduces a new hazard. | The Act requires **traceability of modifications** (Art. 11). Regulators must see how changes affect compliance. | • Keeping change logs only in internal JIRA tickets, not mirrored in the technical file.
• Forgetting to evaluate risk after a non‑functional change (e.g., UI redesign). | --- ## 3. Data Management | **Item** | **What must be documented** | **Why it is required** | **Common mistakes companies make** | |----------|----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------| | **3.1 Data sources & acquisition** | • Origin of each dataset (internal, public, third‑party).
• **Legal basis** for data collection (consent, contract, public domain).
• **Date of acquisition** and **frequency of updates**. | Annex IV (point c) demands a **complete data‑lineage** to verify that the data respect fundamental rights (e.g., GDPR) and that the system’s performance is based on appropriate material. | • Citing “open‑source data” without linking to the exact version/URL.
• Ignoring data‑source licensing restrictions. | | **3.2 Dataset composition** | • **Training, validation, and test set** description (size, class distribution, demographic breakdown).
• **Sampling strategy** (random, stratified, oversampling).
• **Labeling procedure** (human annotators, guidelines, inter‑rater agreement). | Enables assessment of **bias, representativeness and robustness**. The Act explicitly requires that data be **relevant, accurate, and representative** (Art. 10). | • Using a single “master” dataset for all phases without documenting the split.
• Not reporting demographic sub‑group sizes, which hides potential bias. | | **3.3 Data preprocessing & augmentation** | • Algorithms applied (normalisation, encoding, feature

evaluations.prompt: An AI medical imaging system achieves 95% accuracy in lab testing but degrades to 82% with real-world data (different equipment, lighting, patient demographics). What do EU AI Act Article 15 accuracy and robustness requirements mean for this system? What must the company do?
evaluations.model_response:
## 1. Quick TL‑DR | EU AI Act (Article 15) requirement | What it means for your imaging AI | Immediate actions you need to take | |-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------------| | **Accuracy** – the system must meet the performance level claimed to users and that is appropriate for the intended purpose. | The lab‑only 95 % figure can no longer be the “official” claim if real‑world performance is 82 %. The AI is a **high‑risk medical device** (AI‑based “medical imaging analysis”) and must demonstrate that the accuracy it delivers in the environments where it will be used is at least the minimum level required by the relevant medical device standards (e.g., IEC 62304/62366, EU MDR 2017/745). | • Re‑measure accuracy on a representative, *in‑the‑wild* data set (different scanners, lighting, demographics).
• Set a **publicly documented performance target** (e.g., ≥ 90 % sensitivity & specificity, or a clinically‑validated diagnostic‑value).
• If the target cannot be met, either improve the model or restrict the intended use. | | **Robustness** – the system must continue to work reliably when faced with foreseeable variations (different hardware, lighting, patient groups, etc.). | The 13‑point drop (95 % → 82 %) tells regulators that the AI is **not robust** to the variations that occur in everyday clinical practice. The Act expects you to have demonstrated that the model is tolerant to those variations **before** you place it on the market. | • Systematically test the AI across the full range of expected operating conditions (scanner models, image acquisition protocols, patient ages/ethnicities, ambient light, compression artefacts, etc.).
• Document the “robustness envelope” (the set of conditions under which the AI still meets the accuracy target).
• Implement technical safeguards (domain‑adaptation, data‑augmentation, out‑of‑distribution detection, fallback to human review). | --- ## 2. The EU AI Act – Article 15 in Plain English | Clause (AI Act) | What the law requires | Why it matters for a medical‑imaging AI | |------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------| | **15(1) Accuracy** – “the AI system shall achieve the level of accuracy stated by the provider, which shall be commensurate with the intended purpose.” | • You must **prove** that the AI delivers the accuracy you advertise **in the conditions of actual use**.
• The claim must be **transparent**, documented in the technical file, and **not overstated**. | In a diagnostic setting, an overstated accuracy claim can lead to mis‑diagnoses, patient harm, and regulatory non‑compliance. | | **15(2) Robustness** – “the AI system shall be robust against foreseeable variations in the operating environment and shall maintain its performance within the stated limits.” | • Identify **all foreseeable sources of variation** (hardware, software, user interaction, patient demographics, lighting, etc.).
• Demonstrate, by testing, that performance stays inside the **pre‑defined tolerance band** for each variation. | Your system’s 13‑point accuracy loss shows the current model is **not robust** to real‑world scanner/lighting/patient changes. | | **15(3) Documentation** – “the provider shall keep evidence of the tests performed, the data used, the methods, and the results.” | • A **complete test‑report** (datasets, splits, metrics, statistical analysis) must be part of the technical documentation submitted to the Notified Body. | Without a traceable test dossier you cannot obtain the CE mark for the AI‑medical device. | | **15(4) Post‑market monitoring** – “the provider shall continuously monitor the AI system’s performance and take corrective actions if the accuracy or robustness fall below the declared level.” | • You need a **real‑world performance monitoring plan** (KPIs, drift detection, incident reporting). | The 82 % figure you observed is exactly the kind of drift the Act expects you to catch and remediate. | > **Bottom line:** The Act does **not** accept “lab‑only” validation. You must prove that the AI works **as claimed** in the **real‑world environments** where it will be deployed, and you must have a process to keep it that way. --- ## 3. How the Act Classifies Your System | Criterion | Result | |-----------|--------| | **AI system used in health care** (medical imaging analysis) | **High‑risk AI** (Annex III, point 8). | | **Intended purpose** – assist radiologists / provide diagnostic suggestion | Falls under **Medical Device Regulation (MDR) 2017/745** as a **software as a medical device (SaMD)**. | | **Consequences** | • Must undergo **CE marking** via a **Notified Body** (conformity assessment).
• Must comply with **MDR technical documentation** *and* **AI Act Article 15** (accuracy, robustness, transparency, post‑market monitoring). | Because of the dual‑regulation nature (MDR + AI Act), the **accuracy & robustness evidence** you produce for Article 15 will also satisfy the MDR requirement for **clinical evaluation** and **performance validation**. --- ## 4. What the Company Must Do – Step‑by‑Step Compliance Roadmap Below is a practical, ordered checklist that you can turn into a project plan (typical timeline 6‑12 months for a high‑risk AI‑medical device). ### 4.1. Define the **Performance Claim** (the “accuracy” you will advertise) | Action | How to do it | Deliverable | |--------|--------------|-------------| | a) Identify the clinically relevant metric(s) (sensitivity, specificity, AUC‑ROC, F1, etc.) | • Consult clinical experts & target‑use guidelines (e.g., BI‑RADS, WHO imaging standards).
• Choose the metric that matters most for the intended decision (e.g., “detect malignant lesions with ≥ 90 % sensitivity at ≤ 5 % false‑positive rate”). | **Performance Specification Document** (PS‑001) | | b) Set the **minimum acceptable level** (the “threshold” the AI must never drop below) | • Base it on clinical guidelines, state‑of‑the‑art literature, and risk analysis (e.g., a 5 % drop in sensitivity may be unacceptable). | **Threshold Table** (accuracy ≥ X % for each metric) | | c) Publicly document the claim (user manual, CE label, website) | • Exact wording, metrics, and reference population. | **Transparency Statement** (T‑001) | ### 4.2. Create a **Robustness Matrix** (all foreseeable variations) | Variable | Example values | How to test | |----------|----------------|-------------| | **Scanner hardware** | Siemens SOMATOM, Philips Ingenuity, GE Revolution, low‑cost portable X‑ray | Gather image sets from each model; run the AI; record performance. | | **Acquisition protocol** | Dose levels (low/standard/high), reconstruction kernels, contrast‑enhanced vs. non‑contrast | Same as above; also test with protocol‑shifts. | | **Lighting / ambient conditions** (relevant for bedside ultrasound, point‑of‑care cameras) | Bright room, dim room, glare, shadows | Simulate with image‑augmentation or capture real data. | | **Patient demographics** | Age groups (pediatric, adult, geriatric), skin tones, body habitus, disease prevalence | Stratify the test set; ensure each stratum meets the threshold. | | **Image quality artefacts** | Motion blur, metal artefacts, compression, noise | Add synthetic artefacts or use real low‑quality scans. | | **Software environment** | OS versions, GPU/CPU types, inference library versions | Run the model on each environment; check for numeric drift. | **Deliverable:** **Robustness Matrix (RM‑001)** – a table that lists each variable, the test range, the observed performance, and the *acceptance criteria* (must stay within the accuracy thresholds defined in 4.1). ### 4.3. Conduct **Comprehensive Validation on Representative Real‑World Data** | Phase | Description | Key Points | |-------|-------------|------------| | **4.3.1. Data collection** | • Acquire a **multicenter dataset** that mirrors the intended deployment (different hospitals, scanners, patient mixes).
• Include at least **30 %** of the data from “non‑lab” sources (per MDR guidance on external validation). | Document provenance, consent, GDPR compliance. | | **4.3.2. Dataset split** | • **Training set** (if you need to retrain) – keep separate.
• **Validation set** – for model‑selection.
• **Test set** – *locked

evaluations.prompt: Design a post-market monitoring system for an AI medical device under EU AI Act Article 12. Include: passive vs active monitoring, data collection requirements, how to detect bias drift, what triggers a reportable incident, and how to coordinate with existing pharmacovigilance.
evaluations.model_response:
## Post‑Market Monitoring (PMM) System for an AI‑Based Medical Device  
**Scope:** AI software that is a “medical device” (e.g., AI‑driven diagnostic, triage, or treatment‑planning tool) and therefore falls under the **EU AI Act (Article 12)** and the **Medical Device Regulation (MDR/IVDR)**. The system is designed to satisfy the AI‑Act’s “post‑market monitoring obligations” while harmonising with the existing **pharmacovigilance (PV) / medical‑device vigilance** infrastructure of the manufacturer.

---

### 1.  High‑Level Objectives  

| Objective | Why it matters for the AI Act | How it is measured |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| **Safety & performance continuity** | Article 12 requires “continuous monitoring of the functioning of the AI system” | Clinical performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, etc.) remain within pre‑defined limits |
| **Bias / fairness surveillance** | The AI Act explicitly calls for “monitoring for bias, discrimination and drift” | Disparity indices (e.g., demographic parity, equalized odds) stay within tolerance |
| **Transparency & traceability** | Obligations to keep a “record of the monitoring activities” | Version‑controlled logs, audit‑trail, and a public Summary of Post‑Market Monitoring (SPMM) |
| **Rapid incident handling** | “Report high‑risk incidents” to the national competent authority (NCA) within 15 days | Incident detection → classification → reporting workflow |
| **Integration with pharmacovigilance** | Many AI‑medical devices are used together with drugs or other devices, and the manufacturer already has a PV system | Shared case‑management platform, harmonised terminology (MedDRA, SNOMED‑CT) |

---

### 2.  Architecture Overview  

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                     Post‑Market Monitoring Hub                |
|   (centralised, ISO‑13485 / ISO‑14971 compliant platform)     |
|                                                               |
|  ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────┐  ┌───────┐ |
|  │  Passive Data Lake   │   │   Active Monitoring │  │  AI   │ |
|  │  (real‑world data)   │   │   Engine            │  │  Bias│ |
|  └─────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────┘  └───────┘ |
|          │                         │                 │        |
|          ▼                         ▼                 ▼        |
|  Data Ingestion Layer  ←─►  Real‑time Analytics  ←─►  Drift‑Detection |
|          │                         │                 │        |
|          ▼                         ▼                 ▼        |
|  Reporting & Escalation ──► Incident Management ──► PV Interface |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

* **Passive Data Lake** – stores all routine, non‑interventional data (EHR, imaging archives, device logs, user‑feedback).  
* **Active Monitoring Engine** – runs scheduled or trigger‑based checks (e.g., every week, after every 1 000 predictions).  
* **Bias‑Detection Module** – applies statistical and ML‑drift techniques on sub‑populations.  
* **Incident Management & Reporting** – maps detected anomalies to the EU AI Act “high‑risk incident” taxonomy and generates the 15‑day report.  
* **PV Interface** – shares cases with the existing pharmacovigilance system (e.g., safety database, signal‑detection tools).

---

### 3.  Passive vs. Active Monitoring  

| Dimension | Passive Monitoring | Active Monitoring |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Trigger** | Data are collected **as‑is** from routine clinical use (no extra action required from users). | System initiates **additional checks** (e.g., periodic re‑training, simulated cases, “challenge sets”). |
| **Data source** | Real‑world data (RWD): EHR, PACS, RIS, device logs, user‑reported outcomes, call‑center tickets. | Synthetic or curated test‑batches, prospective clinical audits, “shadow mode” runs on a subset of live cases. |
| **Frequency** | Continuous, but analysis may be batch‑processed (e.g., nightly). | Defined schedule (weekly, monthly) or event‑driven (after a software update, after a regulatory change). |
| **Goal** | Detect **unexpected trends** (e.g., a gradual performance drop, new adverse event patterns). | Proactively **stress‑test** the AI model, verify that bias mitigation remains effective, confirm compliance after upgrades. |
| **Regulatory fit** | Satisfies Article 12 “continuous monitoring” – no user burden. | Demonstrates “active post‑market surveillance” required for high‑risk AI (e.g., periodic safety update). |

**Implementation Tips**

* **Passive** – Deploy a **FHIR‑based ingestion pipeline** (or HL7‑v2 for legacy systems) that pushes de‑identified data into the lake within 24 h of generation.  
* **Active** – Use a **CI/CD‑style validation pipeline**: after each software version is released, automatically run the AI model on a *validation set* representing all protected groups and compare metrics against baseline.

---

### 4.  Data‑Collection Requirements  

| Data Type | Source | Minimum Content | Retention (per MDR/AI Act) | GDPR Safeguards |
|-----------|--------|----------------|----------------------------|-----------------|
| **Patient demographics** | EHR, registration | Age, sex, ethnicity, socioeconomic proxy (e.g., zip‑code), disability status | 10 years (or longer if required by national law) | Pseudonymisation; explicit consent for secondary use; DPA‑approved processing agreement |
| **Clinical context** | Order entry, imaging request | Indication, comorbidities, prior therapies | 10 years | Same as above |
| **Device output** | AI software logs | Timestamp, version, prediction (probability score, class), confidence interval, decision support text | 10 years | Integrity‑protected logs (digital signatures) |
| **Ground‑truth/reference** | Radiology report, pathology, follow‑up visit | Final diagnosis, treatment outcome, adverse event (AE) coding (MedDRA) | 10 years | Linked to pseudonymised patient ID |
| **User interaction** | UI logs, help‑desk tickets | Action taken (accept/reject AI suggestion), reason for override, time‑to‑decision | 5 years | Store only aggregated statistics where possible |
| **Hardware/environment** | Device metadata | Firmware version, operating system, sensor calibrations, network latency | 5 years | Not personal data – store as is |
| **Adverse events** | PV system, clinician reports | AE description, severity, outcome, causality assessment | 10 years (or as per MDR) | Full GDPR compliance (subject‑access rights, etc.) |

**Key Practices**

1. **Standardised vocabularies** – use **SNOMED‑CT** for clinical concepts, **LOINC** for lab/imaging, **MedDRA** for AEs, **ISO‑3166‑2** for geographic data.
2. **Data‑Quality checks** – completeness (>95 % for mandatory fields), plausibility (age‑diagnosis consistency), timestamp validation.
3. **De‑identification pipeline** – apply **k‑anonymity** (k ≥ 5) for any dataset extracted for analysis; maintain a **link‑key** in a highly‑controlled vault.
4. **Audit‑trail** – every ingestion event logs source, date/time, checksum, and validation outcome.

---

### 5.  Detecting Bias Drift  

Bias drift = **change over time** in the fairness/performance relationship between protected groups (e.g., gender, ethnicity, age).  

#### 5.1  Core Metrics  

| Metric | Definition | Target/Threshold |
|--------|------------|-----------------|
| **Overall performance** | Sensitivity, specificity, AUROC, F1‑score | Within ±5 % of pre‑market baseline |
| **Group‑wise performance** | Same metrics computed per protected group | Δ between any two groups ≤ 10 % (or as defined in the risk‑management file) |
| **Disparity indices** | Demographic parity difference, equalized odds difference, calibration‑slope difference | ≤ 0.1 (absolute) |
| **Statistical parity ratio** | P(pred = positive|group A) / P(pred = positive|group B)
evaluations.judge_reasoning: The model's response demonstrates a strong understanding of the EU AI Act, MDR, and the complexities of post-market monitoring for AI medical devices. The structure, use of tables, and inclusion of specific technical details (FHIR, MedDRA, SNOMED-CT, k-anonymity, ISO standards) are excellent and indicative of expert knowledge. The sections on "High-Level Objectives," "Architecture Overview," "Passive vs. Active Monitoring," and "Data-Collection Requirements" are comprehensive, accurate, and well-reasoned, covering the prompt's requirements for passive vs. active monitoring and data collection in detail. The coordination with pharmacovigilance is also adequately addressed. However, the response suffers from a critical flaw: it is incomplete. The section on "Detecting Bias Drift" cuts off abruptly in the middle of a table, leaving key information missing. Furthermore, while "Incident Management" is mentioned in the architecture and objectives, the prompt specifically asked "what triggers a reportable incident." The response does not provide a dedicated or detailed explanation of these triggers, beyond general mentions of "detected anomalies" or "high-risk incidents." This omission is significant for a post-market monitoring system design, as defining clear triggers is crucial for compliance and safety. **Accuracy:** The information provided is highly accurate and aligns with regulatory requirements and best practices for AI in healthcare. **Completeness:** This is the primary weakness. The abrupt cutoff and the lack of specific detail on incident triggers make the response incomplete. **Reasoning Quality:** The reasoning for the parts that are present is excellent, demonstrating a deep understanding of the subject matter. **Safety & Compliance:** The approach outlined, where complete, strongly adheres to EU AI Act principles, emphasizing continuous monitoring, bias surveillance, transparency, and integration with existing vigilance systems. GDPR safeguards are also appropriately addressed. Due to the critical incompleteness of the response, particularly regarding bias drift detection and the lack of specific triggers for reportable incidents, the model fails to fully address the task prompt, despite the high quality of the content that was provided.

evaluations.prompt: A startup develops an AI recruitment tool. What registration requirements apply? Must they register in the EU database? What information must be provided? What's the timeline? What happens if they deploy without registration?
evaluations.model_response:
**Short answer** | Question | Answer | |----------|--------| | Is the AI‑recruitment tool subject to the EU AI‑Act registration? | **Yes.** A system that “evaluates or creates a profile of a natural person for the purpose of recruiting, hiring, promotion, or termination” is listed in Annex II of the AI‑Act as a **high‑risk AI system**. | | Must the provider register it in the EU‑wide AI‑database? | **Yes.** Before the system can be placed on the market or put into service in the EU, the provider must submit a **conformity‑assessment report and a summary of the technical documentation** to the **European AI Register (EAI‑R)**. | | What information has to be uploaded? | See the “Information to be provided” section below (≈ 15 data fields). | | When must the registration be completed? | • **For new systems** placed on the market **after the AI‑Act becomes applicable** – registration must be **finished before the first use** (i.e. before the system is offered to any EU user).
• **For systems already on the market when the AI‑Act enters into force** – providers have a **24‑month transition period** to bring the system into compliance and register it. | | What happens if the tool is deployed without registration? | • The national supervisory authority can **order a withdrawal, a temporary ban, or a recall** of the system.
• **Administrative fines** of up to **€30 million or 6 % of worldwide annual turnover** (whichever is higher).
• Exposure to civil liability (e.g., discrimination claims, GDPR fines) because the system will be operating without the mandatory risk‑management and transparency safeguards. | Below is a detailed, step‑by‑step guide that a startup can follow to meet the registration obligations, the exact data that must be supplied, the timeline, and the legal fallout of non‑compliance. --- ## 1. Why a recruitment AI is “high‑risk” The AI‑Act (Regulation (EU) 2021/0109) classifies AI systems into three buckets: | Category | Definition | Example | |----------|------------|---------| | **Unacceptable risk** | Banned outright (e.g., social scoring by governments). | – | | **High risk** | Systems that may affect **fundamental rights** or **safety** and are used in specific sectors (biometric identification, critical infrastructure, education, employment, etc.). | **AI‑based recruitment / CV‑screening, candidate ranking, automated interview analysis**. | | **Limited / Minimal risk** | Subject only to transparency or voluntary codes. | Chat‑bots for general information, recommendation engines for movies. | **Annex II, point 2.1** of the AI‑Act explicitly lists *“AI systems used for recruitment, selection, promotion, or termination of employment”* as high‑risk. Therefore the startup must treat the tool as a high‑risk AI system. --- ## 2. Registration requirement (Article 61 & Annex III) ### 2.1 Who must register? | Entity | When does it apply? | |--------|---------------------| | **Provider** (the startup that makes the system available) | Always, if the system is placed on the EU market or put into service in the EU. | | **Importer** (if the tool is supplied by a third‑party outside the EU) | Must ensure the provider has complied. | | **Distributor** | Must verify that the system is registered before distribution. | | **EU Representative** (if the provider is non‑EU) | Must act on behalf of the provider for the registration and for communication with authorities. | > **Bottom line:** If the startup expects any EU‑based employer (or a candidate in the EU) to use the tool, it must register the system in the **European AI Register (EAI‑R)**. ### 2.2 What must be submitted? The register collects a **public “summary of the technical documentation”** plus a **private conformity‑assessment dossier**. The exact fields are listed in Annex III (point 2) of the AI‑Act; the Commission has published a **template UI** (2024) that the startup will fill online. | # | Data field (public) | Why it matters | |---|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Provider name, legal address, EU‑representative (if any)** | Identify the responsible legal entity. | | 2 | **System name / model version** | Enables traceability of each released version. | | 3 | **Intended purpose & use‑case** (e.g., “automated pre‑screening of CVs for mid‑senior software‑engineer roles”) | Shows the high‑risk function. | | 4 | **Target user group** (HR professionals, hiring managers, candidates) | Clarifies who interacts with the AI. | | 5 | **Geographical scope** (EU countries where the system will be offered) | Determines jurisdiction. | | 6 | **Risk classification** (high‑risk – AI‑Act Annex II) | Triggers the registration requirement. | | 7 | **Conformity‑assessment procedure** (self‑assessment / notified‑body) | Shows the assessment path taken. | | 8 | **Result of the conformity assessment** (CE‑marking of AI, EU declaration of conformity) | Proof that the system meets the AI‑Act requirements. | | 9 | **Summary of the technical documentation** (≤ 5 pages) covering:
• System architecture
• Data‑sets used (training, validation, testing)
• Performance metrics (accuracy, false‑positive/negative rates)
• Risk‑management measures
• Human‑in‑the‑loop

The step-by-step guide is particularly helpful for a startup. However, there are a few inaccuracies that prevent a higher

Below is a **ready‑to‑use audit checklist** that you can hand to the AI‑hiring company (or use as a worksheet during an on‑site interview). For every EU‑AI‑Act article you flagged, the table tells you: | **Article** | **(1) What the article makes *applicable* to a hiring‑AI system** | **(2) Evidence the provider must be able to produce** | **(3) Key questions to ask the provider (and, where relevant, the downstream user)** | **(4) Red‑flags that usually indicate non‑compliance or an area that needs deeper investigation** | |-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------| | **5 – Prohibited AI practices** | • The Act bans:
 • AI that manipulates human behaviour in a way that causes physical or psychological harm (e.g., subliminal persuasion).
 • AI that exploits vulnerabilities of specific groups (e.g., people with disabilities).
 • AI that enables social scoring.
• For a hiring tool, the only “prohibited” risk is if the system deliberately manipulates candidates (e.g., nudges them to accept a job they are unfit for) or exploits protected‑group vulnerabilities. | • A **policy statement** (internal code of conduct) that lists prohibited practices and confirms none are used.
• **Design documentation** showing the system does not contain any “subliminal” or “manipulative” functions.

2. Are any of the model’s outputs used to score candidates for purposes other than selection (e.g., for credit, insurance, or social ranking)?
3. Have you performed a specific check for exploitation of vulnerabilities (e.g., disability, age, neuro‑diversity)? | • Any marketing material that claims the AI “optimises candidate motivation” or “boosts acceptance rates” through behavioural tricks.
• Presence of a “scoring” module that is later shared with third parties for non‑employment purposes.
• Absence of a documented prohibition of the listed practices. | | **6 – High‑risk AI systems** | • AI used for **recruitment, selection, and placement of workers** is expressly listed as a high‑risk AI system (Annex III).
• Therefore the full high‑risk compliance regime (risk‑management, data‑governance, documentation, conformity assessment, post‑market monitoring, etc.) applies. | • **Classification decision** (internal memo) that shows the system was mapped to the high‑risk list.

• Evidence that the system is registered in the EU AI‑registry (if required by the Member State). | 1. How did you determine that your product falls under the “recruitment & selection” high‑risk category?
2. Which conformity‑assessment route did you follow (self‑assessment with a notified body, or full third‑party assessment)?

• For hiring, the data must reflect **the labour‑market distribution** and **protected‑group balance** (gender, ethnicity, age, disability). | • **Data‑set inventory** (catalogue of all data sources, dates, versioning).
• **Data‑sheet for each data set** (as per “Datasheets for Datasets” or similar) covering provenance, sampling method, cleaning steps, bias‑mitigation actions, and consent/legality.
• **Statistical analysis** proving representativeness (e.g., demographic parity between training data and the target labour market).
• **Data‑processing agreements** with third‑party data providers confirming GDPR compliance. | 1. What data sources are used for training the model (CVs, LinkedIn profiles, assessment results, etc.)?
2. How do you verify that the data are representative of the whole applicant pool (including under‑represented groups)?
3. What steps have you taken to detect and mitigate bias (e.g., re‑weighting, adversarial debiasing)?

evaluations.prompt: An AI facial recognition system is deployed at airport security. What cybersecurity threats are unique to AI systems? What does Article 15(3) require? How should the company handle model theft, adversarial attacks, and data poisoning threats?
evaluations.model_response:
## 1. AI‑specific cybersecurity threats When you move from a “classical” IT system (firewalls, authentication, patching) to an **AI‑driven** system such as a facial‑recognition pipeline, you inherit a whole new attack surface. The most relevant threats for an airport‑security deployment are listed below, grouped by the stage of the AI life‑cycle they target. | **AI life‑cycle phase** | **Threat class** | **What the attacker tries to achieve** | **Typical manifestation in a facial‑recognition system** | |--------------------------|------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------| | **Data collection & labeling** | **Data poisoning / back‑door injection** | Corrupt the training set so the model learns wrong or hidden behaviours. | An insider (or compromised camera feed) injects images of a “target” person with a subtle pattern that later allows the attacker to appear as an authorised traveller. | | **Model training** | **Training‑time attacks** (gradient leakage, membership inference, model inversion) | Extract private attributes of individuals used in training or discover whether a given person’s data was used. | An external researcher queries a “model‑as‑a‑service” API and reconstructs the face of a high‑profile traveller from the model’s gradients. | | **Model storage & distribution** | **Model theft / extraction (model‑stealing)** | Re‑create the proprietary model (architecture + weights) to reuse, weaponise or sell it. | An attacker scrapes the API, repeatedly sends queries, and builds a surrogate model that replicates the airport’s recognition accuracy. | | **Model inference (runtime)** | **Evasion / adversarial examples** | Slightly perturb an input image so the model mis‑classifies it while the perturbation is invisible to humans. | A malicious passenger wears a printed adversarial pattern on a hat that makes the system think they are a different passenger (or “unknown”). | | **Model deployment & APIs** | **Model‑exfiltration / side‑channel leakage** | Pull model parameters via timing, cache‑side‑channel, or inference‑output analysis. | An attacker measures response latency for different facial poses and infers internal feature embeddings. | | **Post‑deployment monitoring** | **Model drift / data‑drift attacks** | Feed the system with crafted “clean‑looking” data that slowly degrades performance. | A coordinated group of travellers uploads subtly altered selfies to a self‑service kiosk, causing the model to drift toward a biased decision boundary. | | **Supply‑chain** | **Trojaned libraries / compromised frameworks** | Insert hidden malicious code that can be triggered later (e.g., a back‑door that only activates for a specific face). | The facial‑recognition SDK shipped with a hidden trigger that, when presented with a specific QR‑code pattern, disables the liveness check. | | **Governance / compliance** | **Privacy‑by‑design violations** (e.g., lack of differential privacy, inadequate audit trail) | Enables regulatory breaches (GDPR, ePrivacy, ICAO security standards). | The system logs raw facial images without encryption, exposing them to insider theft. | ### Why these threats are *unique* (or at least amplified) for AI systems | **Traditional IT threat** | **AI‑specific amplification** | |----------------------------|--------------------------------| | **Unauthorized access** (e.g., stealing a password) | **Model extraction** – even read‑only API access can be enough to reconstruct the whole model. | | **Malware** (e.g., ransomware) | **Data poisoning** – the “payload” is the training data itself, which can silently corrupt the model for months before detection. | | **Denial‑of‑service** | **Adversarial evasion** – a single crafted image can render the system useless for a particular target without disrupting the whole service. | | **Information leakage** (e.g., logs) | **Membership inference / model inversion** – attackers can infer whether a specific person’s face was ever in the training set, violating privacy. | | **Insider threat** | **Back‑door activation** – insiders can plant a hidden trigger that only fires under very specific visual conditions, invisible to normal testing. | --- ## 2. What does **Article 15(3) GDPR** require? **Article 15** is the *right of access* of the data subject. Paragraph 3 (the “copy” provision) obliges the data controller to: 1. **Provide a copy** of the personal data that is undergoing processing **free of charge** (unless the request is manifestly unfounded or excessive). 2. Deliver the copy **in a structured, commonly used and machine‑readable format** (e.g., JSON, CSV, XML). 3. **Accompany the copy with information** that enables the data subject to understand the data, including: - the purposes of the processing, - the categories of data, - the recipients or categories of recipients, - the envisaged period of storage, - the existence of the right to rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, and to lodge a complaint, and - **the existence of automated decision‑making, including profiling**, and, **where applicable, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject** (Art. 22‑23). > **Bottom line for an AI facial‑recognition system:** > If a traveller (or any other data subject) asks for access, you must give them not only the raw image(s) that the system holds about them, but also a *clear, non‑technical explanation* of how those images are used by the AI model (including any profiling or automated decision‑making). The copy must be in a format that the subject could, if they wished, feed into their own analysis tools. --- ## 3. How to handle the three AI‑specific threats Below is a **practical, layered‑defence playbook** that maps each threat to concrete technical, organisational, and legal controls. The recommendations assume the system is already in production at a high‑security airport environment, so they are calibrated for **high‑availability, low‑latency** operation while still meeting GDPR and aviation‑security standards (e.g., ICAO Annex 17). ### 3.1. Model Theft / Extraction | **Control** | **What it does** | **Implementation tips** | |-------------|------------------|---------------------------| | **Model encryption at rest & in transit** | Prevents attackers who gain filesystem or network access from reading the binary weights. | • Use hardware‑rooted keys (TPM/SGX) to encrypt model files.
• TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication for any API traffic. | | **Secure enclave / Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)** | Runs inference inside a hardware‑isolated zone; the model never leaves the enclave in plaintext. | • Deploy the model on Intel SGX, AMD SEV, or ARM TrustZone.
• Use remote attestation so the airport’s security operations centre can verify the enclave is running the expected code. | | **API rate‑limiting & query‑budgeting** | Limits the number of inference queries a single client can make, throttling the data needed to reconstruct the model. | • Token‑bucket per API key (e.g., max 1000 queries per hour).
• Enforce “prediction‑only” responses (no confidence scores, embeddings, or gradients). | | **Output sanitisation** | Removes side‑channel information that could be used for model extraction. | • Return only the final decision (e.g., “Match / No‑Match”).
• Mask or quantise confidence scores (e.g., “high / medium / low”). | | **Watermarking & fingerprinting of model parameters** | Allows you to prove ownership if a stolen model appears elsewhere. | • Embed a secret “digital watermark” in the weight matrix (e.g., via a specialised loss term).
• Keep a secure registry

evaluations.model_response:
**Short answer**

| Role | Who is covered by the EU AI Act? | Which national authority will take the lead in market‑surveillance? | What language versions must be supplied? |
|------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **US‑based AI‑hiring provider** | Yes – as a *provider* that places an AI system on the EU market (or puts it into service) it falls under the Act. It must appoint an EU legal‑representative. | The authority of the Member State where the EU‑representative is established (usually the Member State of the *first* user). In this set‑up the **German** authority will be the lead, with coordination from France and Spain. | All mandatory information (CE‑marking, conformity‑assessment docs, user‑manual, transparency notice, risk‑assessment, etc.) must be available in **German, French and Spanish** (the official languages of the Member States where the system is placed in service). |
| **German company (the “user”)** | Yes – as a *user* of a high‑risk AI system it must ensure the system complies and keep the required documentation. | Same lead authority (Germany). | Same three language versions (German, French, Spanish). |
| **French job‑market AI developer (if a distinct provider)** | Yes – as a *provider* of a component that is part of the overall system, it is also subject to the Act. | French authority will be involved as a “co‑ordinating” authority, but the **lead** remains the German authority because the system is first put into service for the German user. | French language is mandatory for the component; the full system still needs German & Spanish copies. |
| **Workers in Spain (persons affected)** | They are the “persons affected” under the Act; any breach that harms them may be reported to the **Spanish** market‑surveillance authority, which can request cooperation from the lead (German) authority. | Spanish authority has *secondary* jurisdiction (right to act on complaints, request information, and cooperate with the lead authority). | Spanish translation is required for all user‑facing and safety‑critical information. |

Below is a more detailed step‑by‑step explanation of **why** each of those answers holds under the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/____, “AI Act”).  

---

## 1.  Who is subject to the EU AI Act in this scenario?

### 1.1  The US‑based AI‑hiring company – **Provider**
* **Legal definition (Art. 3(1)(b))** – A *provider* is any natural or legal person who places an AI system on the market or puts it into service in the Union, irrespective of where the provider is established.
* The US company supplies the AI‑hiring system to a German enterprise. By doing so it **places the system on the EU market** (the German company can freely use it in the Union) and **puts it into service** in the Union.
* Consequently, the US provider is **directly subject to the AI Act**.  
  * Obligations: conformity‑assessment, CE marking (if high‑risk), post‑market monitoring, a quality‑management system, a “technical documentation” file, a “user‑information” sheet, a “risk‑assessment” and a “post‑market monitoring plan”, etc.
* **EU Representative (Art. 4(2))** – Because the provider has no establishment in the EU, it must designate a **legal representative** established in a Member State. The representative is the point of contact for the authorities and must keep the technical documentation “accessible” in the Member State of its establishment.

### 1.2  The German company – **User**
* **Legal definition (Art. 3(1)(c))** – A *user* is a natural or legal person that uses an AI system for its intended purpose.
* The German firm is the first *user* of the system. If the system is classified as **high‑risk** (which a hiring‑AI almost certainly will be, because it affects persons’ access to employment), the user also has **specific obligations** (Art. 10‑12):
  * Ensure the system is used only as intended, keep the provider’s documentation, perform a **conformity‑assessment** (or rely on the provider’s CE marking), maintain a **logbook**, and conduct **human‑oversight**.
* The user can be held liable for breaches of the Act (e.g., using a non‑compliant system).

### 1.3  The French job‑market AI component – **Second Provider (optional)**
* If the French AI is a **separate, independently marketed AI system** that is integrated into the overall hiring solution, its developer is also a *provider* under the Act.
* The French provider must likewise have an EU representative (it may already be established in France, so its own national authority is its “home” authority).

### 1.4  Workers

evaluations.prompt: A company develops a high-risk AI system for biometric access control. Do they need a notified body? If so, when and what exactly must the notified body assess? What happens if the notified body and company disagree?
evaluations.model_response:
**Short answer**

*Yes – a notified body will be required **only** if the biometric‑access‑control AI system is a *safety component* of a product that falls under another EU product‑legislation (e.g. a door‑lock that is a “regulated product”).*  

If the AI system is a *stand‑alone* high‑risk AI (i.e. it is not a safety component of a regulated product), the provider can fulfil the conformity‑assessment obligations by an **internal (self‑assessment) procedure**; a notified body is **not** mandatory in that case.

When a notified body is needed, it must assess the provider’s **quality‑management system (QMS) and the AI system itself** against **all mandatory requirements of the AI Act** (risk‑management, data‑governance, robustness & accuracy, transparency, human‑oversight, post‑market monitoring, etc.) **before** the system is placed on the EU market or put into service.

If the notified body and the provider disagree on conformity, the AI Act provides a **dispute‑resolution cascade**: the provider may request a second opinion from another notified body, and the national competent authority can intervene, possibly ordering a repeat assessment, suspending the CE‑marking or imposing corrective measures. The provider may also challenge the authority’s decision before the courts, but the product cannot be marketed until the disagreement is resolved in favour of conformity.

Below is a step‑by‑step breakdown of the legal framework, the exact moments when a notified body is involved, the scope of its assessment, and the procedural rules for handling disagreements.

---

## 1.  Where does the AI Act place a “biometric access‑control” system?

| AI system | Annex III (high‑risk) | Annex IV (conformity‑assessment) |
|----------|-----------------------|----------------------------------|
| **AI for biometric identification / verification** (e.g. facial‑recognition, fingerprint, iris, voice) used to grant or deny physical access | **Listed** – high‑risk AI (Article 6(1) & Annex III) | **Internal control** *unless* the system is a **safety component** of a regulated product (Annex IV‑1) or is a **remote biometric identification system** used in real‑time (Annex IV‑2). |

*Biometric access‑control* is therefore a **high‑risk AI system**. Whether a **notified body** must be involved depends on the second column:

| Situation | Need for a notified body? | Legal basis |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------|
| Stand‑alone AI software that decides “grant/deny” and is installed on a generic door‑lock that is **not** a regulated product (e.g. a simple electronic strike) | **No** – internal conformity assessment is sufficient. | AI Act, Art. 9(1) & Annex IV‑1 (internal control). |
| AI embedded in a **product that is regulated** (e.g. a door‑locking mechanism that is a **“product with a safety function”** under the Machinery Directive, the Low‑Voltage Directive, the Construction Products Regulation, etc.) | **Yes** – third‑party assessment by a **notified body** is mandatory. | AI Act, Annex IV‑1 (QMS & third‑party assessment for safety‑component AI). |
| AI used for **remote biometric identification** (e.g. live facial‑recognition cameras scanning a crowd from a distance) | **Yes** – notified‑body assessment (Annex IV‑2). | AI Act, Annex IV‑2 (RBI systems). |

> **Bottom line for the question:** *If the access‑control AI is just software that runs on a generic lock, the company can self‑declare conformity. If the lock itself is a regulated product (e.g. a safety‑critical e‑lock covered by the Machinery Directive) the AI becomes a **safety component**, and a notified body must be involved.*

---

## 2.  When must the notified body be engaged?

| Step | What happens | Timing (relative to market placement) |
|------|---------------|--------------------------------------|
| **1. Design & development** | Provider establishes a **quality‑management system (QMS)** that complies with the AI Act (ISO 9001‑compatible) and prepares **technical documentation** (risk‑assessment, data‑governance, testing reports, user‑manual, post‑market monitoring plan, etc.).
evaluations.judge_reasoning: The model provides a comprehensive and generally accurate response to the prompt, demonstrating a strong understanding of the EU AI Act, particularly concerning high-risk AI systems, notified bodies, and conformity assessment procedures. **Strengths:** 1. **Completeness:** The response addresses all parts of the prompt: whether a notified body is needed, when, what it assesses, and what happens in case of disagreement. 2. **Accuracy (Detailed Sections):** The detailed tables and explanations in sections 1, 3, and 4 are highly accurate. It correctly identifies biometric access control as a high-risk AI system and correctly outlines the two main scenarios requiring a notified body: when the AI is a safety component of a regulated product (Annex IV-1) and when it's a remote biometric identification (RBI) system (Annex IV-2). The scope of assessment (QMS, AI system itself, all AI Act requirements) and the dispute resolution cascade are also very well-explained and accurate. 3. **Clarity and Structure:** The use of a short answer, followed by a step-by-step breakdown with tables, significantly enhances readability and comprehension. 4. **Reasoning Quality:** The reasoning is sound and well-grounded in the AI Act's provisions. **Areas for Improvement / Minor Inaccuracies:** 1. **Inconsistency in Summaries:** The primary weakness lies in the "Short answer" and the "Bottom line for the question" summaries in Section 1. * The "Short answer" states: "Yes – a notified body will be required **only** if the biometric‑access‑control AI system is a *safety component*..." This is inaccurate because a notified body is *also* required if the system is a remote biometric identification (RBI) system, regardless of whether it's a safety component. The model correctly identifies this in the detailed table but fails to reflect it in the initial summary. * Similarly, the "Bottom line for the question" states: "If the access‑control AI is just software that runs on a generic lock, the company can self‑declare conformity." This is only true if that software is *not* an RBI system. If it's a live facial recognition system on a generic lock, it would still require a notified body. This omission in the summary could be misleading. 2. **"When" Section Focus:** Section 2, "When must the notified body be engaged?", while informative, describes the overall process and provider's actions more than specifically highlighting the *moments* of notified body engagement. While it mentions "before the system is placed on the EU market" and "periodic surveillance audits," it could be more direct in listing the specific interaction points for the notified body. Despite these minor inconsistencies in the summary statements and the slight deviation in the "when" section's focus, the core information provided in the detailed sections is robust and correct. The fine is calculated on a sliding scale (see “How the fine is worked out”). | Expect a fine that can run into the tens of millions for a €500 m turnover company. | | **How the fine is calculated** | Factors listed in Art. 61(2) – nature, duration, intent, mitigation, repeat offences, size of business, etc. | Most Member States apply a “base fine” (e.g., 2 % of turnover for a first‑time serious breach) and then adjust up/down with multipliers (0.5‑2). | Run the numbers now – a €200 m turnover firm could face a €4 m‑€12 m fine for a first breach, rising to €30 m‑€120 m if aggravating factors apply. | | **Can private individuals sue?** | The AI Act does **not** create a direct “private right of action”, but Art. 71 gives a **right to compensation** from the provider/operator. | National civil‑law rules (product‑liability, consumer‑protection, GDPR‑style damage actions) allow affected persons to bring a tort claim. | Yes – a consumer, employee or competitor can sue the *company* (and, in some jurisdictions, its directors) for damages caused by the illegal AI. | | **Maximum fine** | €30 million **or** 6 % of worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher (Art. 61(1)). | Some Member States have “cap‑plus” provisions (e.g., Germany can add a surcharge of up to 20 % of the base fine for aggravating circumstances). | The ceiling is EU‑wide; national authorities cannot exceed it, but they can add the national surcharge. | | **Directors’ personal liability** | Not explicit in the AI Act. • breach of fiduciary duties under national company law,
• possible criminal negligence (if the breach is “intentional” or “reckless”),
• civil liability for damages (Art. 71). | • **France** – “responsabilité pénale du dirigeant” for “faute grave” in compliance.
• **Germany** – § 93 AktG (director liability) and § 30 StGB (negligent endangerment) can be invoked.

Penalties – the statutory ceiling ### 2.1. The statutory maximum > **Article 61(1) AI Act** > “Member States shall lay down the rules on penalties applicable to infringements of this Regulation. The penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive. The maximum fine shall be **€30 million or 6 % of the total worldwide annual turnover of the undertaking concerned in the preceding financial year, whichever is higher**.” *The “maximum fine” is therefore a **hard ceiling** that no Member State may exceed.* ### 2.2. How the fine is *actually* computed Article 61(2) lists the **six statutory aggravating/mitigating factors**: | Factor (Art. 61‑2) | Typical national weighting (example) | |-------------------|--------------------------------------| | **Nature, gravity and duration** of the infringement | Base multiplier 1‑2 (e.g., 1.5 for “serious but short‑lived”, 2 for “systemic”). | | **Intentional or negligent** character | 1.5‑2 if intentional; 1‑1.2 if only negligent. | | **Degree of cooperation** with the authority | 0.5‑1 (cooperation can halve the fine). | | **Previous infringements** (repeat offence) | 1.5‑2 for 2nd offence, up to 3 for 3rd. | | **Size and economic capacity** of the undertaking | 0.5‑1 (small SMEs may get a reduction). | | **Mitigating circumstances** (e.g., rapid remedial action) | 0.5‑0.8. | Most Member States (Germany, France, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands) have published **“penalty tables”** that translate these factors into a **percentage of the base fine**. The *base fine* is usually set at **2 % of worldwide turnover for a first serious breach**. #### Example calculation (illustrative, not binding) | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Worldwide turnover (FY‑2025) | €500 million | | Base fine (2 % of turnover) | €10 million | |

evaluations.prompt: Design a risk management system for a high-risk AI system under EU AI Act Article 9. Include: risk identification, risk analysis, risk evaluation, risk mitigation, and how risks must be updated through the lifecycle. For each phase, provide specific examples.
evaluations.model_response:
## Risk‑Management System (RMS) for an **EU AI‑Act Article 9 – High‑Risk AI system** Below is a full‑cycle RMS that satisfies the obligations of **Annex III** of the AI Act (the “risk‑management system” requirements) and mirrors ISO 14971‑style processes that regulators are already familiar with. The design is illustrated with a concrete **example** – an **AI‑driven medical‑diagnostic support system (MDSS)** that classifies radiology images and provides triage recommendations. The same structure can be re‑used for any other Article 9 system (e.g., AI‑based safety components in autonomous vehicles, AI‑controlled industrial robots, AI‑enabled biometric identification for law‑enforcement, etc.). --- ### 1. Governance & Documentation Backbone | Element | What it is | Who owns it | Frequency | |--------|------------|------------|-----------| | **Risk‑Management Plan (RMP)** | High‑level roadmap (scope, responsibilities, timelines, tools, acceptance criteria). | **Risk Manager** (often the Compliance Officer) | Created at project start; updated on major design changes. | | **Risk Register (RR)** | Structured list of all identified risks (ID, description, source, likelihood, impact, risk level, mitigation, owner, status, review date). | **Risk Owner** (data scientist, security lead, UX lead, etc.) | Live document; reviewed each development sprint and at every release. | | **Technical Documentation (TD)** | Annex II‑style dossier (system description, data lifecycle, training pipeline, performance metrics, testing results, risk‑management evidence). | **Product Owner / Documentation Lead** | As‑built for each version; submitted for conformity assessment. | | **Post‑Market Monitoring Plan (PMP)** | Procedures for collecting performance, safety, and incident data after deployment; defines KPIs, reporting channels, and review cycles. | **Post‑Market Surveillance (PMS) Team)** | Starts at CE‑marking; updated quarterly or after any incident. | | **Change‑Control Log (CCL)** | Records of every software, data‑set, or hardware change and its impact on risk. | **Configuration Management Lead** | Every change request. | > **Tip:** Store all artefacts in a secure, version‑controlled repository (e.g., GitLab + Confluence) with role‑based access and audit trails – this satisfies the AI Act’s “traceability” requirement. --- ## 2. RMS Phases & Activities ### 2.1 Risk Identification | Step | Activity | Tools / Artefacts | Example (MDSS) | |------|----------|-------------------|----------------| | **2.1.1 Define System Boundaries** | List all functions, inputs, outputs, interfaces, operating environment, user groups, and external dependencies. | System Architecture Diagram, Use‑Case Matrix | • Input: DICOM images from CT/MRI scanners.
• Output: “High‑risk”, “Medium‑risk”, “Low‑risk” recommendation.
• Interface: PACS, EMR, clinician UI. | | **2.1.2 Identify Hazard Sources** | For each boundary element, brainstorm possible hazards (technical, organisational, societal). Use **HAZOP**, **FMEA**, or **Checklist** (bias, safety, privacy, security, robustness, explainability, environmental impact). | Hazard‑Identification Checklist (see Annex A), FMEA worksheet | • **Data‑bias** – training set under‑represents pediatric patients.
• **Model‑robustness** – adversarial perturbations cause mis‑classification.
• **Human‑in‑the‑Loop failure** – UI hides uncertainty, leading to over‑reliance.
• **Security** – ransomware on the inference server disables triage. | | **2.1.3 Capture in Risk Register** | Create a new RR entry for each hazard with a unique ID, source, and initial severity/likelihood placeholders. | RR template (ID, Description, Source, etc.) | **R‑001** – “Bias against under‑represented age groups”. | | **2.1.4 External Reference Check** | Map identified hazards to relevant standards (ISO 27001, ISO 14971, IEC 62304, GDPR, EDPB guidelines). | Standards matrix | Bias → ISO 20785 (AI‑Bias); Security → ISO 27001; Safety → IEC 62304. | --- ### 2.2 Risk Analysis | Step | Activity | Tools / Artefacts | Example (MDSS) | |------|----------|-------------------|----------------| | **2.2.1 Determine Likelihood** | Estimate frequency of occurrence (e.g., *Rare*, *Unlikely*, *Possible*, *Likely*, *Almost Certain*) based on data, past incidents, testing results. | Probability scoring matrix, historical incident database. | For **R‑001** (age bias) → *Possible* (training data contains 5 % pediatric cases). | | **2.2.2 Determine Severity (Impact)** | Evaluate consequences on health, safety, fundamental rights, environment, or financial loss. Use **EU‑AI‑Act severity categories** (Minor, Moderate, Serious, Critical). | Severity matrix, clinical impact assessment. | Mis‑diagnosis of a malignant tumor → *Critical* (life‑threatening). | | **2.2.3 Compute Risk Level** | Combine likelihood and severity (e.g., a 5‑point risk matrix) → **Risk Rating** (Low, Medium, High, Unacceptable). | Risk matrix (see Annex B). | **R‑001** → *Possible* × *Critical* = **High** (needs mitigation). | | **2.2.4 Document Evidence** | Attach data sources, test results, expert opinions that justify scores. | Test reports, data‑set statistics, expert interviews. | Bias analysis report showing 30 % lower sensitivity for patients < 12 y. | | **2.2.5 Prioritise** | Sort RR by risk rating; create an **Action‑Priority List**. | Sorted RR view. | Top‑3 risks: R‑001 (bias), R‑002 (adversarial robustness), R‑003 (human‑over‑reliance). | --- ### 2.3 Risk Evaluation | Step | Activity | Acceptance Criteria | Example (MDSS) | |------|----------|----------------------|----------------| | **2.3.1 Define Acceptable Risk Thresholds** | Set organisational limits (e.g., *No Critical risk may remain unmitigated; High risks must be reduced to Medium or lower*). | Policy document, signed by senior management. | – Critical risks must be eliminated or transferred.
– High risks must be reduced to Medium. | | **2.3.2 Compare Measured Risk vs. Threshold** | For each RR entry decide: **Acceptable**, **Acceptable with Controls**, **Unacceptable**. | Decision matrix. | **R‑001** (High) → **Unacceptable** → requires mitigation. | | **2.3.3 Residual Risk Assessment** | After envisaged mitigations, re‑estimate likelihood/severity to produce **Residual Risk**. Document the *risk‑benefit justification* required by Article 9 (
